risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The study is the second part of a randomized controlled trial of 54 children and adolescents with JIA ... the randomization process has been described in detail [23]." and "After written consent by the parents and assent by the children, the subjects were randomized into an exercise or a control group. The person carrying out the group allocation was blinded." Comment: This paper states that participants were randomized, but it does not describe the actual method used to generate the allocation sequence (e.g. computer-generated list, random numbers, blocks). Blinding of the person carrying out allocation relates to concealment, not sequence generation. Although it refers to an earlier report for detailed randomization methods, no direct description is provided here, so sequence generation is not confirmable from this report alone.

Quote: "After written consent by the parents and assent by the children, the subjects were randomized into an exercise or a control group. The person carrying out the group allocation was blinded." Comment: This indicates some blinding of the allocator, but the paper does not explicitly describe a concealment mechanism such as central allocation, pharmacy control, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes. Therefore, it is not possible to confirm that the person enrolling participants could not foresee upcoming assignments before enrolment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The participants fulfilled a training programme three times a week for 12 weeks." and "after written consent by the parents and assent by the children, the subjects were randomized into an exercise or a control group." Comment: The intervention was a home-based exercise programme versus control, so participants and personnel supervising/delivering the programme would necessarily know group assignment; there is no indication that participant/personnel blinding was possible. The study also reports outcomes that are plausibly influenced by knowledge of allocation, including self-reported pain (VAS, CHAQ/CHQ pain domains), well-being/quality of life questionnaires, and perceived exertion (Borg scale). Therefore lack of blinding could affect outcomes, giving high risk of performance bias.

Quote: "The same physiotherapist, who was blinded to the previous measurement, performed all measurements." Quote: "The Child Health Assessment Questionnaire (CHAQ) is a questionnaire..." "The Child Health Questionnaire (CHQ-C87) is a survey..." and "The children were asked to report if pain occurred during the test occasions... documented with a 10 centimetres visual analogue scale (VAS)." Comment: Outcome assessors included both a clinician/physiotherapist for performance measures and the patients themselves for questionnaire and pain VAS outcomes. The physiotherapist was only stated to be blinded to previous measurement, not to treatment allocation. In addition, the study included subjective self-reported outcomes (CHAQ, CHQ, pain VAS), where the patient is the outcome assessor. Because this exercise trial was not described as blinding participants, and self-reported pain/QoL outcomes are vulnerable to knowledge of allocation, the worst-outcome rule gives overall High risk.

Quote: "There were 10 dropouts from the control group after the randomization, as they had preferred to belong to the exercise group. There were another six dropouts after the first test occasion due to the families’ lack of time." Quote: "Out of the 54 participants 48 completed the training, but there were dropouts in some of the measurements." Quote: "35 children fulfilled the CHAQ and 39 the CHQ at all test occasions." Quote: Table 7 shows "CHAQ n=20" for exercise and "n=15" for control; CHQ domains use "n=23" for exercise and "n=16" for control. Comment: Post-randomization attrition/exclusion was substantial and imbalanced. For the questionnaire outcomes of interest, only 35/54 (64.8%) had CHAQ data at all test occasions, i.e. 35.2% missing overall, and CHQ was 39/54 (72.2%), i.e. 27.8% missing overall. The paper also reports 10 post-randomization dropouts in the control group due to preference for exercise, creating clear arm imbalance/reasons related to allocation, and analysed denominators differ by outcome without robust missing-data handling reported. This is positive evidence of high attrition bias risk.
